name: System Design — Storage & Caching
description: Databases, indexes, sharding, replication, cache patterns, eviction, stampede mitigation, CDN headers.

cards.definition: Relational data with stable schema, transactional integrity, complex queries with joins. ACID guarantees, mature tooling. Examples: financial records, order systems, anything with foreign-key relationships.
Stores hierarchical/JSON-like documents. Flexible schema, good for nested data and content systems. Examples: MongoDB, CouchDB.
Fast lookups by exact key. Limited query flexibility (no range, no secondary index in many implementations). Examples: Redis, DynamoDB, etcd.
Optimized for high write throughput across many nodes; rows have variable columns indexed by row key + column key. Strong with time-series and event data. Examples: Cassandra, HBase, ScyllaDB.
Optimized for relationship traversal — friend-of-friend, recommendations, fraud rings. Examples: Neo4j, Neptune, JanusGraph.
Default in most relational DBs. A balanced tree supporting equality and range queries efficiently. Updates rebalance in place; reads are O(log n). Good general-purpose.

Log-Structured Merge-tree. Writes go to an in-memory memtable, then are flushed and merged into sorted files on disk. Optimizes write throughput at the cost of read amplification. Used by Cassandra, RocksDB, LevelDB.
Maps each term to the list of documents containing it. Foundation of full-text search engines (Elasticsearch, Lucene). 'rate limiter' → document IDs containing those terms.
Assign rows by key range (user IDs 0–1M on shard 1, 1M–2M on shard 2). Simple, supports range queries. Risk: hot spots if data isn't evenly distributed (e.g. recent timestamps all on one shard).
Apply hash(key) and assign by hash value. Distributes data evenly. Range queries are expensive (data is scattered). hash(key) % N is brittle to node count changes.
A lookup service maintains key → shard mappings. Flexible, supports non-uniform distribution. Cost: the directory itself becomes a bottleneck and SPOF.

Maps both nodes and keys onto a ring. A key is assigned to the first node clockwise. When a node is added/removed, only ~1/N keys are remapped (vs full remapping with hash%N). Used by DynamoDB, Cassandra, memcached.

All writes go to a single leader; followers replicate the WAL. Reads from leader (linearizable) or followers (stale). Simplest model. Bottleneck: leader writes; failover blocks writes. Examples: Postgres streaming replication, MySQL.

Multiple leaders accept writes (often per-DC). Local writes are fast; cross-region replication is async. Hard part: conflict resolution — LWW, CRDTs, or manual merge. Used in active-active deployments.
Any node accepts writes; W of N must ack. Reads contact R nodes; with R+W>N, latest data is guaranteed. Conflicts via vector clocks + read-repair. Examples: Cassandra, DynamoDB, Riak.
Every write goes through Raft/Paxos and is committed once a majority of nodes ack. Linearizable but writes are bound by quorum-RTT latency. Examples: etcd, CockroachDB, Spanner.
App checks cache; on miss, reads DB and populates cache. Writes: update DB then invalidate cache. Default choice; flexible. Trade: stale entries until TTL or invalidation.
App talks only to the cache; cache fetches from DB on miss. Simpler app code, less control over the read path. Same staleness semantics as cache-aside.
App writes to cache; cache writes synchronously to DB. Strong consistency at the cost of slower writes; cache must be up. Best for read-mostly workloads with consistency needs.
App writes to cache; cache async-flushes to DB. Very high write throughput; data loss possible on cache crash. Best for tolerable-loss workloads (counters, metrics).
Evict the entry not accessed for longest. Matches temporal locality; cheap implementation (doubly-linked-list + hashmap). Weakness: a single full-scan can flush hot entries.
Evict the entry with the lowest access count. Hot set survives scans. Weakness: stale entries with high historical counts keep 'winning'; implementation is complex.
When a popular cache entry expires, many requests miss simultaneously and all hit the DB. Mitigations: single-flight locking, probabilistic early expiration, background refresh (SWR), TTL jitter.
Only one request fetches from DB on miss; others wait and reuse the result. Adds latency for waiters; needs distributed lock if cache is shared. Canonical Redis impl: atomic Lua check-and-set.
Add random variance to cache TTLs so entries don't all expire at the same instant. Cheap defense against synchronized stampedes — useless if a single hot key is the problem.

Background job refreshes entries before they expire; reads always hit the cache (sometimes serving slightly stale data) while a refresh runs. Eliminates miss latency at the cost of always-stale possibility.
Embed a version in the key (user:42:v3). Old entries expire naturally; no invalidation logic needed. Great for immutable assets and content addressable by hash.
max-age=N — cache for N seconds (browser + CDN). s-maxage=N — CDN-only TTL. no-store — never cache. private — browser-only, never CDN. Pair with ETag for conditional 304 revalidation.

A fingerprint (hash) of the response body returned by the server. Clients send it back as If-None-Match; server returns 304 Not Modified (no body) if unchanged. Saves bandwidth without sacrificing freshness.

Layered: client (in-memory, sub-ms, per-user) → CDN/edge (5-50 ms, global, static) → app in-process (sub-ms, per-instance) → distributed (Redis, ~1 ms, cluster-wide) → DB query cache. Combine layers for the right tradeoff.

Followers serving reads to scale read throughput beyond what the leader can handle. Reads may be slightly stale (replication lag). Don't use for read-your-writes consistency without sticky routing or follower-read fences.
Command Query Responsibility Segregation — separate the model that handles writes from the model that serves reads. Lets each scale and be optimized independently. Often paired with event sourcing.
Precomputed result of an expensive query, stored as a table. Read-time cheap, write-time refresh (eager, lazy, or scheduled). Trades freshness for read speed.

Duplicate or embed data to avoid joins on the read path. Faster reads; writes must update multiple places (more complex, risk of inconsistency). Common in NoSQL, often combined with async fan-out updates.
A 4-node cluster still requires 3 nodes for quorum (same as N=3) but adds a node that can fail. Even sizes don't improve fault tolerance — they just cost more. Run consensus clusters at N=3, 5, or 7.
